posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked:
• 2007 Delamotte Champagne Blanc de Blancs Millésimé - France, Champagne
    This is a simple, but very well executed champagne. It’s not too complex, but all the hallmarks of a nice wine are here. Classic chardonnay flavours, with a little bit of apple and citric acidity, counterbalanced by a fairly sweet mousse and a light chalkiness. I’m not totally sure that this comes off to me as Salon-lite, but it’s pretty good all the same. (93 pts.)


  • 2005 Henri Boillot Corton-Charlemagne - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Corton-Charlemagne Grand Cru
    Of the Corton Charlies tonight, this had the most reticent nose. There was still a minute hint of reduction here, as well as a little bit of oak, and the fruit characteristics here were a little muted. The palate was also surprisingly closed. I thought there would be much more exuberance and fleshiness from a 2005 chardonnay, but it turns out this is about as taciturn as a 2005 Corton rouge. The true form of this wine seems to be ripe and fat, but right now it’s still tightly bound in a straitjacket of some sort. However, despite the riper fruit here, there’s still a good vein of acidity. (93 pts.)
  • 2008 Henri Boillot Corton-Charlemagne - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Corton-Charlemagne Grand Cru
    Probably my least favourite of the Corton Charlemagne flight, this had the darkest colour of all of the wines. I broached the subject of botrytis with Henri, who promptly dismissed it. Still, I’m not convinced that this was as clean of a wine as he made it out to be. There seems to be some botrytis influence with the darker colour and a hint of orange and honey on the nose. The palate is a bit softer as well, and the fruit shows a lot more golden than white (which is more typical for the rest of the wines). It’s a bit sweet and sappy, and reminds me a little bit of apple juice here. While this is also clearly a fatter wine, it doesn’t have quite the same amount of cut as the later iterations. (93 pts.)
  • 2013 Henri Boillot Corton-Charlemagne - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Corton-Charlemagne Grand Cru
    This was my favourite of the Corton Charlemagne flight (and Henri’s as well). It shows quite a bit of reduction (although the 2016 shows even more), but it’s very clean, and doesn’t have any sulphuric stench. This is also the first wine where the oak use hasn’t yet seemed to fully integrate, but it’s so well done that that is hardly a distraction. The palate is clean and racy, with tons of white-fruited acidity, and an intense mineral focus. It’s expressive, but not yet fully so. You can almost feel the wine is ready to unfurl. It’s still a little tight now, but time in the glass did help. (93 pts.)
  • 2016 Henri Boillot Corton-Charlemagne - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Corton-Charlemagne Grand Cru
    If I thought that the 2013 was reduced, this brought it to a whole new level. The latest iteration of this wine feels extremely primary, and there’s still some bubbly fruit that hasn’t yet settled underneath the oak and flint. In fact, the fruit is still slightly sweet. I would expect this to tighten up forther in the next few months, but this is an incredibly delicious package already. This is a wine in great balance, and I think the only reason that this wasn’t my wine of the flight was because this was just too damn young. I’m guessing that with time, this will win out over the great 2013. (93 pts.)


  • 2016 Henri Boillot Pommard 1er Cru Les Rugiens - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Pommard 1er Cru
    As far as the reputation of Pommard goes, this is a wine that doesn’t fit the mold at all. Perhaps it’s because Rugiens is on the opposite side of the town as the most famous of Pommard vineyards (Epeneaux, if you didn’t guess). In fact, this is, perhaps rightly, a little more volnaysien than I thought a Pommard could be. Beautiful lifted florals and red fruit, with a crunchiness and freshness from the bright acids. The tannins are present, but they’re not rustic or heavy, but serve the purpose of reminding you that this wine is actually meant to be aged. (93 pts.)
  • 2014 Domaine Henri Boillot Volnay 1er Cru Champans - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Volnay 1er Cru
    Fanboy I am of Volnay, I actually found the 2016 Rugiens more to my taste than this. I think, in all fairness, that this is a wine caught shutting down, which is why it wasn’t as expressive as it could have been. There are glimmers of red and black fruit here, along with a tannic structure that seems to play a larger role than it ought to be in 2014. Overall, the nose here is great, but the palate needs much more ability to open up again. (93 pts.)
  • 2010 Henri Boillot Clos Vougeot - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Clos Vougeot Grand Cru
    As far as the reds go, this is a clear step up from the two Cote de Beaune reds in the flight. Perhaps it’s the ranking, or the vintage, or something else. In any case, this is a dense and thickly textured wine, with generous amounts of black fruit that are counterbalanced by just the right amount of acidity and tannin. Structural but fleshy, there’s a lot of potential in this wine. There’s a slight bit of earthiness here that adds interest, but by and large, like all the other reds tonight, these are clean, fruit-driven, but not overly polished iterations of red Burgundy. (93 pts.)
  • 2009 Henri Boillot Chambertin - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Chambertin Grand Cru
    A stunning nose greets you when you raise your glass. It’s got everything you would expect from such a wine – intensity, complexity, and power. Perfumed from the red fruit, but earthy at the same time, this is a wine with lots and lots to discover. The fruit, as expected from the vintage, is quite opulent, but it’s all held together by huge tannins and ample acidity. As with so many other 2009s I’ve tasted, the more I taste them, the more I think that the vintage is excellent. The wines have not turned out to be the massive fleshpots that I had been afraid of. Going back to the Chambertin though, there is still a slight touch of oak here, but as with the whites, the oak use is judicious and hardly an impediment to enjoyment. (93 pts.)


  • 2006 Domaine Henri Boillot Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Clos de la Mouchère - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru
    I have never been a particular fan of the 2006 white Burgundy vintage, but I found this bottle to be outstanding all the same. This is the quintessential Henri Boillot wine. It’s got that classic reduction on the nose, followed by the white fruit, minerals, and acidity. The palate here is a little broader than I’m used to in the Mouchere, but really, that’s like saying this is closer to a Corton-Charlemagne. There’s a smokiness here that I also quite enjoy. Extremely youthful for the vintage, at least this bottle shows no sign of deterioration. (93 pts.)
  • 2009 Domaine Henri Boillot Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Clos de la Mouchère - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru
    This is pretty much the same wine that I managed to taste a few weeks ago. It’s a reduction bomb, followed by opulent fruit on the palate, but with a great mineral leanness all the same. And I’ll repeat my mea culpa with respect to the 2009 vintage – I’ve found the wines to be superb most of the time. Surprisingly, despite the ripe quality of the fruit here, I don’t find this wine fat at all. In fact, that linear acidity is classic Puligny. It was a brilliant bottle last time, and is a brilliant bottle this time. (93 pts.)
  • 2010 Domaine Henri Boillot Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Clos de la Mouchère - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru
    I liked the 2009 Mouchere a little more than this one, which I found to be a tinnier, higher-toned, and slightly screechier version of the 2009. I think that without that amount of opulent fruit on the palate, the acidity and minerality seem to stand out more. I also felt that this wine seemed to be a little shut down, which might be responsible for the lack of fruit that I found here. On the other hand, I wouldn’t be racing to trade in my 2010s for 2009s; this is still an outstanding wine, and very much true to what Puligny is. (93 pts.)
  • 2016 Domaine Henri Boillot Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Clos de la Mouchère - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru
    This is almost too young to honestly judge, but I will say that this wine is very much still in the mold of what the Mouchere ought to be. White fruit, lean, racy acidity, with a linear, mineral drive. This bottle feels relatively simpler now, with its bubbly and outgoing white fruit and tinge of sweetness (as you might find from a wine that had just recently been bottled). Tastes a little grapey now, but that’s really a function of its youth; I expect that with a few years, this will easily rank up there as one of the better Moucheres made. (93 pts.)


  • 2007 Domaine Henri Boillot Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Pucelles - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru
    Either no one at the table thought this was premoxed, or everyone was too polite to say something (myself included, I suppose), but this is the third vineyard in 2007 where I’ve found premox in Henri Boillot’s wines. I had brought this bottle as a sacrificial lamb to gauge reactions, but by and large, reactions seemed to be positive. Perhaps that was part of being tied up with the spirit of the evening (which was splendid), but I’m digressing too much. This wine was premoxed. I’ve had plenty of premoxed 2007 Henri Boillots. I’m done with that vintage. (93- pts.)
  • 2016 Henri Boillot Chevalier-Montrachet - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Chevalier-Montrachet Grand Cru
    I haven’t tasted many Chevalier-Montrachets, but this bottle is exactly what I would expect Chevalier to taste like. The intense nose here, with a touch of herbs and reduction milder than the Corton-Charlemagne or Moucheres, is already a monumental thing to behold. There’s oak, but it’s done tastefully in the usual manner. The intensity here is what differentiates this from all the other white wines tonight. It is a focussed, powerful laser beam, with inexorable forward drive. The acidity and minerality all contribute to that. Racy, and even though the concentration here is intense, there is no fat. This is as muscular and sinewy as it comes. And while this is all driving forward, there is a vast expansiveness on the palate that only the top grands crus can accomplish. Peripherally to that linear drive is an incredibly complicated mix of fruit and green herbs. Breathtaking and monumental. (93 pts.)

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
2008 Henri Boillot Corton-Charlemagne - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Corton-Charlemagne Grand Cru
Probably my least favourite of the Corton Charlemagne flight, this had the darkest colour of all of the wines. I broached the subject of botrytis with Henri, who promptly dismissed it. Still, I’m not convinced that this was as clean of a wine as he made it out to be. There seems to be some botrytis influence with the darker colour and a hint of orange and honey on the nose. The palate is a bit softer as well, and the fruit shows a lot more golden than white (which is more typical for the rest of the wines). It’s a bit sweet and sappy, and reminds me a > little bit of apple juice here> . While this is also clearly a fatter wine, it doesn’t have quite the same amount of cut as the later iterations. (93 pts.)

Sounds like it was advanced, not necessarily botrytis. That apple juice note is always the give away to me.

Also, Boillot’s Chambertin is sneaky good, dunno why it doesn’t get more hype, prob cause negotiant? The mouthfeel is always so lithe and the fruit pure and bountiful. Really damn good wine. Even the 11s were good.

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
Sorry replying in piecemeal, was just looking at your scores didn’t pay attention to the words.

2009 Domaine Henri Boillot Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Clos de la Mouchère - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru
This is pretty much the same wine that I managed to taste a few weeks ago. It’s a reduction bomb, followed by opulent fruit on the palate, but with a great mineral leanness all the same. And I’ll repeat my mea culpa with respect to the 2009 vintage – I’ve found the wines to be superb most of the time. Surprisingly, despite the ripe quality of the fruit here, I don’t find this wine fat at all. In fact, that linear acidity is classic Puligny. It was a brilliant bottle last time, and is a brilliant bottle this time. (93 pts.)

09, critics (and everyone else) were prob wrong about how 09 white burg would develop. Definitely shed some of that initial ripe fat and gained a ton of acidity. When you think about it… 07-14… what a time to be alive for white burgundy.

posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked:
c fu:

09, critics (and everyone else) were prob wrong about how 09 white burg would develop. Definitely shed some of that initial ripe fat and gained a ton of acidity. When you think about it… 07-14… what a time to be alive for white burgundy.

Consider me a big fan of 2009 now. On the bright side, it’s not like the prices have gone up much since 2009 release, probably because, like you said, it’s just been a long string of amazing vintages.

I wonder if my fears of 2015 fall along the same lines as 2009. I haven’t been as thrilled with the 2015s I’ve tasted. Do you think they might resolve the way the 2009s have?

The 2016 Boillot lineup (at least, what I tasted here tonight) is something serious and worthy of attention.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
A. So:
c fu:

09, critics (and everyone else) were prob wrong about how 09 white burg would develop. Definitely shed some of that initial ripe fat and gained a ton of acidity. When you think about it… 07-14… what a time to be alive for white burgundy.

Consider me a big fan of 2009 now. On the bright side, it’s not like the prices have gone up much since 2009 release, probably because, like you said, it’s just been a long string of amazing vintages.

I wonder if my fears of 2015 fall along the same lines as 2009. I haven’t been as thrilled with the 2015s I’ve tasted. Do you think they might resolve the way the 2009s have?

The 2016 Boillot lineup (at least, what I tasted here tonight) is something serious and worthy of attention.

Some of the best producers certainly think their 2015s will do that. Many comment that they never saw wines so resistant to oxidation as the '15s were in barrel (e.g. PYCM, Alex Moreau, Sébastien Caillat).

The Clos Vougeot is also quite underrated I would say.

Your notes do a good job of capturing Henri’s manner.

posts.name: Justin S ! n g h
posts.cooked: I had the Delamotte back to back with Salon, and while they are clearly in different leagues, I can see the stylistic resemblance. Then again, I’m sure that could be said of other champagnes.

c fu:

Sorry replying in piecemeal, was just looking at your scores didn’t pay attention to the words.

2009 Domaine Henri Boillot Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Clos de la Mouchère - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru
This is pretty much the same wine that I managed to taste a few weeks ago. It’s a reduction bomb, followed by opulent fruit on the palate, but with a great mineral leanness all the same. And I’ll repeat my mea culpa with respect to the 2009 vintage – I’ve found the wines to be superb most of the time. Surprisingly, despite the ripe quality of the fruit here, I don’t find this wine fat at all. In fact, that linear acidity is classic Puligny. It was a brilliant bottle last time, and is a brilliant bottle this time. (93 pts.)

09, critics (and everyone else) were prob wrong about how 09 white burg would develop. Definitely shed some of that initial ripe fat and gained a ton of acidity. When you think about it… 07-14… what a time to be alive for white burgundy.

If I can pick out one valuable lesson I’ve learned chasing Burgundy of both colors, its to not place a whole lot of stock in the initial opinions of the wines in the modern era from either critics or consumers. Whether its the 09 “They’re too ripe” scare or the 13 “they’re too light!” scare, my experience has been that pedigree almost always shows within a few years.

The 99-01 trio taught me this - I passed on so many 00 reds because “they’re too light,” but the best wines show so well now.

If I can pick out one valuable lesson I’ve learned chasing Burgundy of both colors, its to not place a whole lot of stock in the initial opinions of the wines in the modern era from either critics or consumers. Whether its the 09 “They’re too ripe” scare or the 13 “they’re too light!” scare, my experience has been that pedigree almost always shows within a few years.

Only got so much money. Gotta pick and choose them battles. Fortunately 07-13 white burg was still pretty affordable so I purchased regardless of vintage. Reds on the other hand…

posts.name: Jerry Hey
posts.cooked: Nice set of wines. The Mouchere is my go to white burg annually. It looks like your high score of the night was 93 and your low score of the night was 93. Good way to make people read the reviews…

Burgundy math is complicated math.

Been doing it for a while, and been able to taste and learn from the kind of folks who brought 60’s La Tache to dinner still sporting a $9.99 price sticker. I won’t claim to have all the answers, but I have yet to go wrong betting on the upside of good producers in “off” years with 10+ years in the cellar.

Much as people chant “producer, producer, producer,” they mostly ignore their own advice when “off” years come out, which in my experience makes it a little easier to just buy producers. Once you’re past the first bloom of youth, you have to start thinking about how a wine evolves - so 2000 might be great at 15-20 years, while 2002 might take 25 - big gap…

posts.name: Karl K@zaks
posts.cooked:
I too like Mouchere.

As for the Rugiens, I have wines from there (not Boillot) which were less “Volnaysian” but rather more rugged - what say you who read this?

Personally tasting through the 15’ vintage whites, my initial assumption was that they would be riper, bigger without cut… one of the producers we sell completely obliterated that idea, those 15’s were absolutely stunning with a long life to live and great acid…

posts.cooked:
William Kelley:
A. So:

Consider me a big fan of 2009 now. On the bright side, it’s not like the prices have gone up much since 2009 release, probably because, like you said, it’s just been a long string of amazing vintages.

I wonder if my fears of 2015 fall along the same lines as 2009. I haven’t been as thrilled with the 2015s I’ve tasted. Do you think they might resolve the way the 2009s have?

Some of the best producers certainly think their 2015s will do that. Many comment that they never saw wines so resistant to oxidation as the '15s were in barrel (e.g. PYCM, Alex Moreau, Sébastien Caillat).

The Clos Vougeot is also quite underrated I would say.

Your notes do a good job of capturing Henri’s manner.

William, thank you for the kind words. It means a lot coming from you.

Adding on Jim’s comments below yours, it gives me a sense of ease that maybe the 2015s will indeed lose that fleshiness. Makes me optimistic. When you say “resistant to oxidation”, what do you mean? Did producers put less sulphur in the wines?

I’ve not had many of the Henri Boillot reds, to be honest. I think that may be a bit of a mistake. Certainly, I think of him as more of a white wine producer. As an aside, no one at the table picked up any large stylistic differences between Guillaume’s and Henri’s wines.

Womp womp. We had a shot 2007 a few weeks ago. Even more obvious since there was a flawless 2009 that the table next to us shared.

The 2007 Pucelles at this dinner was shot, and I had a 2007 CC that was shot last year. The 2008 CC seemed a little advanced at the dinner as well (if Charlie’s interpretation of my note is taken to be correct).

In general, I don’t think 2007 and 2008 are developing well. I haven’t noticed problems with any other vintages though.

posts.name: Don Cornutt

posts.name: A Ballin

c fu:
jbray23:

Personally tasting through the 15’ vintage whites, my initial assumption was that they would be riper, bigger without cut… one of the producers we sell completely obliterated that idea, those 15’s were absolutely stunning with a long life to live and great acid…

you trying to get me to get a case of that 15 roulot!?

There was very little trying involved, I had you at Roulot…

A Ballin:

Why bother including scores if it’s just a joke?, distracts from the otherwise good notes.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
Alex Rychlewski:

Hi,

Interesting notes. I prefer Boillot’s whites to his reds by a long shot.

No one else seemed to notice: every single wine was rated 93 points!
How is this possible?

Alex R.

Hi Alex - If you want to know his real scores you can see them on Cellartracker. If you want the story behind the joke feel free to PM me.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
jbray23:
A Ballin:

Why bother including scores if it’s just a joke?, distracts from the otherwise good notes.

There is a whole thread about this already, ok, 2 threads…

Because he’s passive/aggressive enough to want to take the time to post the notes, but piss people off while doing it so they don’t waste their time reading them and responding. I only opened his thread because of the lure of hearing about dinner with Henri Boillot. Alas, there wasn’t a scrap of description of where this was, how Henri was involved, or anything about him or his thoughts in general. And while I’m at it, not only does he make the choice to continue with the asshat 93 points (in the face of nearly 2/3 of the board having told him he’s an asshat for doing so), but now he posts the notes without direct links back to CellarTracker, so if someone did actually want to read the unadulterated notes he has to go to the trouble of going to CT, searching for his CT name (which is posted around here somewhere), and finding this particular set of notes. Thanks, but no thanks.

posts.name: Keith A k e r s

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
A. So:
William Kelley:
A. So:

William, thank you for the kind words. It means a lot coming from you.

Adding on Jim’s comments below yours, it gives me a sense of ease that maybe the 2015s will indeed lose that fleshiness. Makes me optimistic. When you say “resistant to oxidation”, what do you mean? Did producers put less sulphur in the wines?

I’ve not had many of the Henri Boillot reds, to be honest. I think that may be a bit of a mistake. Certainly, I think of him as more of a white wine producer. As an aside, no one at the table picked up any large stylistic differences between Guillaume’s and Henri’s wines.

No, I just mean that the wines didn’t oxidize for a long time when exposed to oxygen.

Guillaume barrel fermented all the 2017 reds, so you may notice a difference soon.

posts.name: Brian Buzzini
posts.cooked:
A. So:
  • 2007 Domaine Henri Boillot Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Pucelles - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru
    Either no one at the table thought this was premoxed, or everyone was too polite to say something (myself included, I suppose), but this is the third vineyard in 2007 where I’ve found premox in Henri Boillot’s wines. I had brought this bottle as a sacrificial lamb to gauge reactions, but by and large, reactions seemed to be positive. Perhaps that was part of being tied up with the spirit of the evening (which was splendid), but I’m digressing too much. This wine was premoxed. I’ve had plenty of premoxed 2007 Henri Boillots. I’m done with that vintage. (93- pts.)

WHOA…what a minute…what does THIS mean??? (93- pts.)

WineBerserkers – 26 Mar 18

I’m 93 points on that [POLL]

A taster gives every wine 93 points. What say you? poll No tasting notes were harmed in the making of this poll.

Stop calling me out, and I’ll stop responding.
